votingPower.howCalculatedTooltip: veQS = QS × (remaining_days / 730). The longer the lock period, the more voting power you get

quorum.explanation: Quorum is the minimum participation needed for a vote to be valid

proposals.pageSubtitle: Vote on proposals that shape the future of Quantum Shield

vote.quorumTooltip: Quorum is the minimum voting participation required for a proposal to be valid. Without reaching quorum, proposals cannot pass regardless of vote outcome.

emptyState.description: There are no proposals in this category

footer.disclaimer: Governance participation is voluntary. Voting does not constitute financial advice. All proposals are subject to Time Lock (7 days) and Council veto rights.

meta.title: {id}: {title} | Quantum Shield Governance
meta.description: View proposal details and participate in voting

timeline.title: Timeline
timeline.timeLockTooltip: Time Lock is a waiting period between proposal approval and execution. During this time, the Security Council can veto the proposal if issues are discovered. This is an important safety mechanism to protect the protocol.

vote.title: Cast Your Vote
vote.quorumTooltip: Quorum is the minimum voting participation required for a proposal to be valid.

voteSuccess.title: Vote Submitted!
voteSuccess.subtitle: Your vote has been recorded on-chain

meta.description: Create a new governance proposal and submit it for community voting

parameter.name: Parameter Change
parameter.description: Modify protocol parameters like bond amounts, fees, or time periods

upgrade.name: Contract Upgrade
upgrade.description: Upgrade smart contracts or add new protocol functionality

council.name: Council Change
council.description: Add or remove Security Council or Purpose Committee members

requirement.title: Proposal Requirement
requirement.description: You need at least {minVeqs} veQS to create a proposal.

step2.title: Proposal Details
step2.summaryPlaceholder:
Describe your proposal in detail.
- Motivation
- Specification
- Security considerations
step2.forumLinkHint: Link to prior discussion on the governance forum

warning.title: Important Notice
warning.text: Once submitted, this proposal cannot be edited or cancelled. The proposal will be subject to community voting for 7 days, followed by a 7-day Time Lock if passed. Security Council and Purpose Committee may exercise veto rights per CP-3.

success.title: Proposal Created!
success.subtitle: Your proposal is now open for voting

meta.title: Council | Quantum Shield Governance
meta.description: View Security Council and Purpose Committee oversight

council.pageSubtitle: Security Council and Purpose Committee oversight

infoBox.title: Council Veto Rights (CP-3)
infoBox.text: The Security Council can veto proposals that pose security risks. The Purpose Committee can veto proposals that violate Core Principles (CP-1 to CP-5). All veto actions require public disclosure of reasoning for transparency (CP-5).

securityCouncil.title: Security Council
securityCouncil.description: Responsible for protocol security. Can execute emergency pause and veto security-related proposals. Requires 4/7 multisig for actions.
securityCouncil.multisigTooltip: Multisig (multiple signatures) means actions can only be executed with approval from multiple members. 4/7 means 4 out of 7 members must sign off.

purposeCommittee.title: Purpose Committee
purposeCommittee.description: Guardians of Core Principles (CP-1 to CP-5). Can veto proposals that violate the protocol's fundamental values and mission.

footer.disclaimer: Council actions are recorded on-chain and publicly verifiable per CP-5 (Transparency).

meta.description: View your voting history, proposals, and delegations

meta.title: Learn Governance | Quantum Shield
meta.description: Learn how Quantum Shield governance works and how to participate

onboarding.title: Learn Governance
onboarding.subtitle: Shape the future of Quantum Shield together

whatIs.title: What is Governance?
whatIs.description: Governance is a system where the community decides Quantum Shield's operational policies. You can vote on important decisions like fee changes, new features, and security measures. Your vote shapes the project's future.
whatIs.highlight: There is no central authority. The community governs together.

getQS.title: Get QS Tokens
getQS.description: First, acquire QS tokens. You can purchase them on DEXs or receive them through airdrops.

lockQS.title: Lock QS to Get veQS
lockQS.description: Lock your QS tokens in Token Hub to receive voting power (veQS). The longer you lock, the more voting power you get.

vote.title: Vote on Proposals
vote.description: Review active proposals and vote for or against. Voting is free, only gas fees apply.

proposals.title: Create Proposals
proposals.description: With enough veQS, you can create your own proposals and ask the community to vote on them.

delegate.title: Easy Delegation
delegate.description: When busy, you can delegate your votes to a trusted representative. Undelegate anytime.

council.title: Security Council
council.description: Proposals with security risks can be vetoed by the council, protecting the protocol.

veqs.title: What is veQS?
veqs.description: veQS (Vote-escrowed QS) is a unit of voting power. You earn it by locking QS tokens, and the amount depends on your lock duration.
veqs.point2: Shorter lock periods give less veQS
veqs.point3: veQS decreases over time (reaches 0 at lock end)

cta.title: Let's Get Started!
cta.description: Lock QS and join governance. Your voice decides Quantum Shield's future.

q1.question: What is governance?
q1.answer: Governance is a system where the community decides Quantum Shield's operational policies. Users with veQS (voting power) can vote on proposals and create new ones. There is no central authority; the community governs together.

q2.question: Can anyone participate?
q2.answer: Yes, anyone with veQS can participate in voting. You can get veQS by locking QS tokens in Token Hub.

q3.question: Does voting cost anything?
q3.answer: Voting itself is free, but you need a small amount of ETH for transaction gas fees. Voting does not consume your veQS.

q1.question: How do I vote?
q1.answer:
1. Select a proposal from the proposals list
2. Review the proposal and choose 'For' or 'Against'
3. Sign with your wallet to confirm your vote
You can change your vote anytime during the voting period.

q2.question: How long is the voting period?
q2.answer: The standard voting period is 7 days. After voting ends, results are finalized. If passed, a 7-day time lock follows before execution.

q3.question: What is quorum?
q3.answer: Quorum is the minimum voting participation required for a proposal to be valid. Without reaching quorum, proposals cannot pass regardless of vote outcome. Quorum varies from 4% to 15% depending on proposal type.

q1.question: How is veQS calculated?
q1.answer: veQS = QS × (remaining_days / 730 days). A maximum 2-year lock gives a 1:1 ratio. For example, locking 1000 QS for 1 year gives approximately 500 veQS.

q2.question: Does veQS decrease over time?
q2.answer: Yes, veQS gradually decreases towards the lock end date. To maintain voting power, you need to extend your lock period or lock additional QS.

q1.question: What is the Security Council?
q1.answer: The Security Council is a group of 7 experts responsible for protocol security. They can pause the system in emergencies and veto proposals with security risks. It operates with 4/7 multisig.

q2.question: What proposals can the council veto?
q2.answer: The Security Council can veto proposals with security risks, and the Purpose Committee can veto proposals violating Core Principles (CP-1 to CP-5). All vetoes require public disclosure of reasoning (transparency principle).

q1.question: What is delegation?
q1.answer: Delegation means entrusting your voting power to a trusted representative. It's useful when you're busy or want someone with expertise to decide for you. Even when delegated, you can always vote yourself, which takes priority over delegation.

q2.question: Can I undelegate anytime?
q2.answer: Yes, you can undelegate or change delegation anytime. When you undelegate, voting power returns to you immediately.

contact.title: Have more questions?
contact.description: If you have unresolved questions, feel free to contact us.
